channel.title: Affect on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Affect on emsenn.net

item.title: Interoception
item.description: Interoception is the sense of the body’s internal physiological state. Where proprioception tells you where your body is in space, interoception tells you what’s happening inside it: heart rate, breathing depth, gut motility, bladder fullness, temperature, hunger, thirst, and the diffuse signals of autonomic arousal that underlie what gets called “feeling.” A. D. Craig argued that interoception constitutes the physiological basis for subjective feeling states — it’s how the body knows its own condition [@craig2002].

item.title: Affective infrastructure
item.description:
Affective infrastructure

Affective infrastructure is a concept associated with Lauren Berlant that names the emotional and procedural frameworks through which people manage ongoing instability. These frameworks include genres of public feeling, modes of narrative resolution, institutional practices of acknowledgment-without-change, and the interpretive labor through which volatile situations are made legible and manageable. Affective infrastructure is not a building or a network; it is the pattern of feeling and response that allows a system to continue functioning without addressing its structural conditions.

item.title: Affective infrastructure
item.description:
Affective infrastructure

Affective infrastructure is a concept associated with Lauren Berlant that names the emotional and procedural frameworks through which people manage ongoing instability. These frameworks include genres of public feeling, modes of narrative resolution, institutional practices of acknowledgment-without-change, and the interpretive labor through which volatile situations are made legible and manageable. Affective infrastructure is not a building or a network; it is the pattern of feeling and response that allows a system to continue functioning without addressing its structural conditions.

item.title: Feeling rules
item.description:
Feeling rules

Feeling rules are a concept developed by Arlie Russell Hochschild in The Managed Heart (1983) to name the social prescriptions that dictate which emotions count as appropriate responses to given situations. Feeling rules are not suggestions or personal preferences; they carry normative force. They tell people not only how to act but what to feel — and how intensely, for how long, and toward whom.

A wedding prescribes joy. A funeral prescribes grief. A job interview prescribes confident enthusiasm. In each case, the prescription is not merely about outward behavior but about the feeling itself. When someone feels the wrong thing — irritation at a wedding, relief at a funeral — they experience guilt, confusion, or the need to manage the discrepancy. This management is what Hochschild calls emotion work: the active labor of bringing one’s feelings into alignment with social expectations.

item.title: On Californication
item.description:
I’m writing this babble because I don’t have any notes on californication and that’s a deficit, after [[id:ea55f339-24a1-49ea-adc6-8786ad86e288][Babble, [2025-09-01 Mon 10:09], Mapping the neurotic platform intellectual]].

Keyword salad

Here’s some random keywords that have to do with californication, until I can come write something proper:

item.title: Radar Range: M*A*S*H TV and the Training Grounds of American Telemetric Affect
item.description:
Title:
Radar Range: MAS*H TV and the Training Grounds of American Telemetric Affect

NOTE: the laugh track is a key part of this - its meta relation as a forced thing from the studios, audiences hate(d) it, etc.

Abstract:
This paper argues that the long-running television series MAS*H (1972–1983) functioned, intentionally or not, as a low-resolution simulation lab for the affective conditions of telemetricism. Operating within the ambient infrastructure of Cold War media, MAS*H trained its viewers to parse war, medicine, bureaucracy, and moral ambiguity not as problems to resolve but as signals to process. Under the guise of satire and sentimentalism, MAS*H anticipated the recursive logic of 21st-century governance: mood as data, narrative as triage, coherence as modulation. This paper situates the show in relation to key thinkers of cybernetic modernity and examines how its structure, character arcs, and audience interactions embodied the nascent logics of affective telemetry.
